adapters.name: conventions
adapters.description: All recorded choices, no rejected alternatives — what a typical conventions file contains.

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
- Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- CI runs on GitHub Actions with a single reusable workflow — One workflow file, matrix per package; no CI vendor lock beyond what the repo host already implies
- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: I propose refactoring our packages/api HTTP layer to fully leverage Hono's native middleware composition, establishing a strict, predictable execution order. We will create a centralized middleware chain where request logging via pino runs first to capture all incoming traffic. Next, an authentication middleware will read the httpOnly cookie and validate the session against our Postgres UNLOGGED table using postgres.js, attaching the user to the Hono context. Finally, our token-bucket rate limiter will execute, utilizing the resolved API key or session, followed by Zod validation at the route boundaries.
acknowledged_rejections: Migrating to Express, Fastify, or another framework for their middleware ecosystems
Using Redis for rate limiting or session caching state

reply.proposal: Although the dashboard state is growing more complex, we should stick to our architectural decision to use Zustand rather than introducing Redux Toolkit. To address the messiness, I propose refactoring our current Zustand setup using the 'slices' pattern to modularize our state into logical domains like session, filters, and toasts. We can also extract complex state derivation and update logic into custom hooks to keep our components clean. This approach maintains our lightweight, low-ceremony state management while providing the necessary structure to scale the dashboard features.

reply.proposal: We will maintain our established convention of using the postgres.js driver with hand-written SQL rather than introducing Prisma. To achieve the requested type safety, we will continue using typed row mappers and Zod schemas to validate and type the results of our SQL queries. This approach maintains our visibility into query plans and avoids adding a heavy runtime abstraction layer, while still ensuring our database access remains fully type-safe.

reply.proposal: I propose using native fetch combined with our existing custom retry wrapper to integrate with the new billing provider's REST API. This aligns with our strategy of avoiding heavy HTTP client dependencies while still providing robust timeout and retry capabilities. We will validate the billing provider's JSON responses at the boundary using Zod schemas to ensure type safety. Any upstream errors will be logged via Pino and mapped into our standard RFC 7807 problem+json envelope for consistent client consumption.

reply.proposal: We must decline the mobile web team's request to make the session token readable from JavaScript, as this violates our security posture against XSS attacks. Instead, we will continue to store session tokens exclusively in httpOnly, SameSite=Lax cookies. To authenticate API requests, the SPA simply needs to configure its native fetch calls with the `credentials: 'include'` or `'same-origin'` option. This allows the browser to automatically attach the secure cookie to outbound requests without the SPA ever touching the raw token.
acknowledged_rejections: Making the session token readable from JavaScript
Storing session tokens in localStorage or sessionStorage
Manually attaching session tokens to fetch headers in the SPA

reply.proposal: To enable unit testing for the request handlers in packages/api, we will use the built-in node:test runner and node:assert rather than introducing Jest. We can test the handlers efficiently by leveraging Hono's app.request() method, which allows us to simulate HTTP requests directly against the router without spinning up a network listener. The tests will be written in TypeScript and executed against the compiled output using node --test, maintaining our zero-dependency testing convention and keeping CI configuration minimal.

reply.proposal: While the new designer prefers component-scoped styles, we will continue using Tailwind utility classes for the new marketing pages to maintain consistency with the rest of the dashboard. We can achieve the desired encapsulation by breaking the UI down into small, reusable React components where the Tailwind classes are scoped strictly to the component's render function. This ensures we avoid runtime style computation, maintain full compatibility with React Server Components, and keep all design tokens centralized in our existing Tailwind config.

reply.proposal: To address the session lookup latency without expanding our infrastructure footprint, we will implement a caching layer using a Postgres UNLOGGED table. We will write a forward-only SQL migration to create this table, which bypasses the write-ahead log to provide near in-memory performance. Our Hono auth middleware will query this table using postgres.js to quickly validate session tokens extracted from the httpOnly cookies. This approach keeps our stack limited to exactly one datastore while achieving read speeds highly competitive with dedicated caching solutions.

- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: Although the task suggests auto-generating migrations, our project conventions strictly mandate hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations to ensure every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL. Introducing a schema definition and diffing tool would violate this principle and our decision to avoid database abstraction layers. Instead, I propose we improve the developer experience by adding a simple Node.js CLI script to scaffold new, correctly numbered SQL migration files, keeping our production and CI history linear while removing the boilerplate of creating the files manually.
acknowledged_rejections: Auto-generated migrations from a schema definition
ORMs or schema diffing tools (e.g., Prisma, Drizzle, Atlas)

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
- Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- CI runs on GitHub Actions with a single reusable workflow — One workflow file, matrix per package; no CI vendor lock beyond what the repo host already implies
- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: To address the mobile team's overfetching concerns while adhering to our REST API strategy, we will implement sparse fieldsets via a ?fields= query parameter. We will update our Zod schemas to validate the fields parameter as a comma-separated list of allowed keys for each specific endpoint. In the Hono route handlers, we will parse this parameter and dynamically filter the JSON response payload to include only the requested fields. To optimize database performance, we can optionally pass these requested fields down to our hand-written SQL queries using postgres.js to only SELECT the necessary columns. This approach maintains our OpenAPI spec as the source of truth and leverages existing HTTP caching mechanisms without introducing new paradigms.
